speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened in January?

speaker: interviewee
text: In January in 1942-- [INAUDIBLE] '42-- '42. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In January '42 was the uprising. Yes, in January '43 that was the [? Stitchnova ?], that's right. The liquidation. They started to liquidate the ghetto, to make the ghetto shorter, shorter, smaller, smaller [? to ?] the-- in January '43. So we were together. You know, coming out, we want to go to work. We heard that the ghettos fool around with the SS and taking [? a lot of ?] people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went up to the upstairs-- last floor, and to the-- Attic?

speaker: interviewee
text: Attic, yeah. Went up to the attic, and we were hiding there. We were laying in that attic fro two, three days until that's quiet down. And then we found out that it's quiet down, so we came down from the attic. And you know, it-- it was cushions with feathers, with everything, you know. We were laying a few days. I came down and remember like now. Then we went down to go to work. The SS men, usually we went to a group. I belong to that group [GERMAN], so we went into the places where the machine-- they have machine and they took another machine and load it-- load it onto the trucks, and they took them back to Germany I belong to the [GERMAN]. And the-- the German, they-- they knew us already, you know, because we came down from the attics, you know, with a feather full. [SPEAKING GERMAN] All right, and then we went, and we worked in that ghetto until-- from the January until the uprising from ghetto I went there. And I went-- this was-- the uprising was in April. Yeah. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what was the effect of the January action on the people who lived in the ghetto? Did that change their attitude or their behavior?

speaker: interviewee
text: More frightened. More frightened. They-- they saw it. Then this is not the play from taking away some people and going to-- to work, which-- which they have said they're sending them to the east to work. But this is an action from liquidation. The liquidation. Then we heard that there's some people coming back, running away from Treblinka and coming back to the-- to the ghetto and telling the stories, you know, what-- what they're doing with the people when they're coming there. So nobody-- nobody could believe it that something like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: But then when we-- we we saw it, see-- see-- what-- where are the people? Why they-- they-- they're not coming some letters when they are sending to the east for work-- to work. They should get some letters from some people from there. And we thought that this is just a play to liquidate, to make shorter [? tie ?] to that ghetto. And-- When you were in Otwock and you heard about Treblinka, you began to believe it.

speaker: interviewee
text: We started to believe, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you came to Warsaw and they said that people were going to work, did you tell anyone that you-- what you knew about Treblinka?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we were talking between ourselves constantly what that's going to be end out of it, the end of it. I see there's no end, completely no end of it. It's the only end is gonna be they're gonna kill us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did--

speaker: interviewee
text: What to do it-- it-- was no way to go out. Where would you go? The ghetto is closed. The train, you cannot go into a train and take a-- a train and go somewhere else.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you have to be, you know, you have to live with the idea maybe-- maybe you're gonna be from the lucky ones. You're gonna remain alive. We knew the end is gonna be-- the end is gonna be liquidation. You see? We-- we heard from Majdanek that time already. We start to hear from Majdanek and from Lublin. We start to hear from some other camps, you know? We know this-- that's not just talking, this-- this is a reality. You know, you see-- you-- you can see them. Lublin-- Were you aware of other activity in the ghetto, resistance activity?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we-- we knew there is. And there was Jewish shows and was everything, you know, was schools for children. You know, but-- they had to do something. The Judenrat was-- tried to do something, and the Jewish-- Jewish organizations tried to do to organize the-- the parents. They should send the kids to-- every-- every place had their group.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about fighting resistance?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the fighting resistances, they started to organize.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who-- who? Who was organizing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Look, they-- it was the Zionist organizations and the Communist organizations and the Bund organizations. All the organizations they belong to-- Jews they belonged there. You see, they start to organize-- organized themselves, and say, what we gonna do? You have to do something, you see. This January action, we didn't do anything. We [? have-- ?] you heard rumors, you know, then something is cooking there. They're organizing themselves. They would not let through that everything like this go out. That's all right. We have to get some resistance. Has to be some resistance.

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- everybody was afraid, you see, to talk even to somebody. You see? Or-- they did it. They organized themselves. Let's say I lived in Simonhof. I moved to Miller 46, another place. The other side was young boys, [? halutism ?], and the Zionists. And we knew-- we knew what they're doing. What they-- they're organizing themselves for some resistance against the German, against the Ukrainian. Wait, were you part of that in any way?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not. I was not belong to any.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the-- and the boys that you were with?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We didn't-- we didn't belong to that group. We went to work, we looked at ourselves. You know. And not-- they didn't took in-- they were afraid even to say by taking into the organizations is only by ear. So-- so you see, you couldn't [? see ?] propaganda openly. We were there in that-- after the-- the January action. We were still remaining, and we moved out from this house. And we moved into Miller 46. We were there and every day we went to work. And we came back, and we live together. Five boys we were there. From the five boys, I think I'm the only one that's left over.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we would live there and, you know, oh the-- the tragic moment came at the uprising from ghetto. You see? And this-- the [INAUDIBLE] of Pesach, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in that house. We prepared ourself for the Passover [? yet. ?] So I can-- we bought a bottle of wine, and we bought a few eggs. And we tried, you see, to have a Passover, a regular Passover.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have matzo?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't have matzo. And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] as we, you know, it came that day of Pesach, the-- the morning. We instead of going out to work, that we heard the ghetto is full around with SS and Ukrainians. We cannot go out. And the gruppenfuhrer, which he took us every day to the-- with the [GERMAN]. He knew some from the leaders from the SS, because he was together with them there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went out and getting up in the morning, we want to go out from the-- from that building. And he said, no, you cannot go out. Because if you gonna go out, you gonna be shot. So we went over to that man which he lived in the same building-- in the same house-- house, and we said, Mr. Wassakela, what we gonna-- his name was Wassakela. We said-- we said, Wassakela, what we have to do? We want to go out to work. We don't want to stay like that. So he said to us, you wait here. I'm gonna go-- I'm not afraid. You see, I'm gonna go out. They know me. And I will find out from the [INAUDIBLE] Konrad-- he knew Konrad, the leader from the SS from the [INAUDIBLE] in ghetto. He knew-- he said, I'm gonna go over to Konrad and ask Konrad what I should do with my people. I have the [INAUDIBLE]. So he went out, and he said, don't move from here. You stay and wait. And about 15, 20 minutes, he came back. He said, I just had an order to take my people and to go over to the [INAUDIBLE]. [INAUDIBLE] was the most-- from the welterfassen.

speaker: interviewee
text: If you came into that street, you were a little bit more secured because they-- the SS and the Ukraine, it they didn't came into that street to take out some people because this was under the jurisdiction from Mr. Konrad, and nobody can come in to that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he came out. He come over to us, and he-- he told us about going out to work that we organize ourselves. We put ourselves about-- about 10 or 15 groups, four-- the four people walking, you know, in a line, and we start to march out. We marched out from 40-- from the-- from the gate from 46, going over to the [INAUDIBLE]. We had to make a right turn going over to the [INAUDIBLE], we have to pass by.

speaker: interviewee
text: As we coming over to corner of Simonof-- Simonhof, we saw the Ukrainians coming from the [INAUDIBLE]-- from the [INAUDIBLE] Simonof. The rifles aimed at us, and they-- you heard them start to shoot. And then finally we heard shooting from the buildings, from the buildings coming up because they thought we are the first group they are starting to take out from the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this started the-- the fight, you know. The-- the [? count ?] and uprising started almost this group when we went out, because the shooting come from the corner of Miller and Simonhof. And we-- everybody start to run. They run there. They run there. I was running, and I came over and they told us to stand-- stay by the wall and pick up the hands. We thought they were going to shoot us. Then this Wassakela came over, said, this is the [INAUDIBLE]. I have to take [INAUDIBLE] to work. So we went into the [INAUDIBLE], and we were there. As soon as he came into the [INAUDIBLE], we were a little bit more security. So you know what-- they have to have-- they have to have-- from Konrad, the-- if Konrad would tell them they can shoot, they shot-- they did it. If that-- they-- they couldn't do it-- they could not do it without his orders. You see? He had to give that order.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were there a whole-- a few days. About two or three days, we were there. And we heard the shooting and the fire. I saw it myself. I went out from the-- from [INAUDIBLE]. I was in the [INAUDIBLE], and we looked up and the buildings we saw the fires. We saw-- and I saw a red flag, the-- the blue-white flag on the-- on a roof, you know, and the building was on fire. You couldn't get out from it. And-- and-- and to come in here, to come into the [INAUDIBLE] and take out the people, they couldn't take out the people. We were there--

speaker: interviewee
text: Then finally came an order we should come out. They're going to make a selection. We have to go over to the [INAUDIBLE] to the military prison that was corner of [INAUDIBLE] and Simonhof. Was-- we came up there. That was the whole-- the whole-- elite from the SS. We saw [? Brandt ?] was there and Konrad was there and some other SS leaders, you know, were there standing and we were together and there were a thousand-- more than a thousand people, you know? And as we were standing there, they brought over from the camp, two SS men brought over a kapo, a man with a girl, and they were carrying a-- a basket in their hand. In the basket, it was-- the bottles with the cocktail-- Molotov cocktails. And there used to be on that corner from the [INAUDIBLE], because I knew it, a toilet during the day. You know you going during the day, the people. It was the-- the the SS man took out one bottle from the-- from the basket, and he throwed it at the wall and it exploded. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were there about two hours. Then there came an order we should go back to the [INAUDIBLE]. We went back to the [INAUDIBLE]. Coming back to [INAUDIBLE], we had to meet over there this is already the fourth-- fifth day in the operation. Don't forget this was the fourth, fifth day. They started bringing some people more. Then came another order, we should come-- go again there opposite the-- the [INAUDIBLE]. And they start to make again a selection. They picked up some people, and the rest we went back to the-- to the-- again to the [INAUDIBLE]. So we were there about eight, nine days in the [INAUDIBLE]. Then they came the order to go up to the umschlagplatz. From the [INAUDIBLE], the umschlagplatz was not too far, so they organized everybody and we went out to the umschlagplatz. We came out to the umschlagplatz. Was hundreds and hundreds and hundreds of people already on the umschlagplatz. They took them out from-- from the other side and they come from [? Schultz ?] and [INAUDIBLE] and from all the factories what they have to organize. And they took them out on the-- on the umschlagplatz. Umschlagplatz, they were standing and selecting the people-- one here, one here, one here. And-- I was standing there. You know, we kept together with [? Yanous ?] [INAUDIBLE], you heard-- you heard the Jewish actor. We were together. We were work-- they were standing together and-- and they started-- they took out from the-- they started take out some people, the SS, 10, 20 to-- to here on this side and this side. And I remained in the back. I remained in the back. I was standing in the back. Then they came for me also. Go on this side. I went to with [? Tuka. ?] We went over the other side and were standing and waiting. Then they took-- they didn't put this into the trains, the cattle trains. They put us in work, that we should go in the Ostbahn. We were working in Ostbahn, loading coals. You know, and over there was barracks, very nice barracks. Some army was there before, and they left the barracks, and we-- you know, we were there-- went there, about a few hundred people, 300, 400 people went in there to that [? hausbarn. ?] And I was between them with these people. And we went-- every day to work, where-- to the railroad. A lot of coals. We would dig it. We had work. We did a lot of work there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they--

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were there in that Ostbahn-- I was there about four or five-- five-- more-- more than five months. Then came the liquidation from the-- from the Ostbahn.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were working on the Ostbahn, did you see the transports leaving?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. This is-- this was a different place completely. The-- the transports which they went to Treblinka, and to Majdanek, or somewhere, that was another-- another rail yard. There was another-- another place. Now, we had-- our duty was to load and unload coals and digging and making rails and all kind of hard work.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were there, in that-- in that-- Ostbahn, I was there, let's say, from '43 from the-- the uprising from the ghetto until [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [? also ?] September-- before September. And one morning, we were still asleep, it was-- I think it was a Sunday morning. It was-- because we were sleeping that time a little longer. We didn't go to work. We looked out [? and see-- ?] what is Ukrainian doing here? What is so many SS doing here? A half an hour later, an order. Everybody out. Take whatever you have and come out. What did you have with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We have a shirt, you know, some-- some-- something to change it with. Where we going? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You will see. You will-- you're gonna go. You're going to work somewhere else. But this was our place. And they put us together, and they took us into the-- to the-- to bahnhof, to the-- the station and, they start to put us into the-- to the trains. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, [? I said, ?] everybody start, oh. Who knows where they're sending us? I think this is how it end already. This is-- this is-- it was [? warm, ?] hot outside, Warsaw, September, and we were there about-- a few hours. And they put-- little-- not just our group. They brought groups from [? Vilanov ?] and-- and some other places. And there was-- they put together I would say it was 1,200, 1,500 people. How many people in each cattle car?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the car, as many-- as many as they could. There was no counting how many. As many they could push in, they pushed it in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you estimate? In your car?

speaker: interviewee
text: My estimation is about 60 people. 55, 60 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: How crowded was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was crowded. You can't-- you have to-- very crowded. And without the toilet. There was no toilet. There was nothing. No water. Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Only standing?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were standing. I-- somebody, you know, pushed himself. He sat down in a corner, and he was sitting. He was afraid they gonna step on him. They put us in the-- in the trains, and you were waiting a few hours, and the train start to move. We said, where are we going? We don't know where we going. One said, we're going to Russia. One said we're going to some other place. All kind of, you know, ideas. And then they stopped it on the road. The training, they started-- train start to move. And it stop somewhere. They-- we looked it up. We asked the SS a little water to drink. He says, oh, you're gonna have water. You're gonna have water. They didn't give anything-- not to eat, not food, not water, nothing, anything. If you have to go, you excuse me, and sit in a corner. You have to-- everything was together, women, and the men and whoever was there. And there was one couple. That-- I see them, a young couple that looks like they had some poison with themselves. And they killed themselves in the-- in the train. They was laying there both together, hugged together. So I see them laying there. And so we were-- instead of-- the whole trip has to take from Warsaw to Treblinka. The maximum is about two hours you could be in there in Treblinka. We were traveling around almost about 20 hours. This place. Stop this place, and this place, and this place, and without anything. Without any food, without anything. We came to that place. Finally, we came, and we come over the train, and we see a sign, Treblinka. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The sign on the station, Treblinka. Everybody's oh. Everybody start to cry, and everybody start to hug each other, and everybody start to kiss each other. I said this is the last [INAUDIBLE]. No-- no more, no more life. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewee
text: And they start to take us out from the-- from the train. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Then we went out of the train. So this kapo, they tell us we should take everything. We took them out, they're dead. They were dead. We have to lay them down, and one SS man jumped on their chest. And he said, [SPEAKING GERMAN]? Why didn't you wait? I should kill you. No, you killed himself.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had to carry them. And we-- see, coming out from the train, we didn't go in Treblinka. We went over to the [? arbeitz ?] Treblinka. From this Treblinka to the [? arbeitz ?] Treblinka was about-- almost about a kilometer. More than a kilometer that we were marching and coming up there. We come up there. They set us down on the ground. We were sitting on the ground about a few hours and waiting until the [GERMAN] came over and they start to make that selection.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Oiben. ?] [? Oiben. ?] [GERMAN]. And with the SS, with Lanz, with the-- with the. Then I know. Even I saw them. I know the names already. From the beginning, I didn't know. Then they start this, this, this, this on this side, and this on this side. They-- from the-- we were there about 1,500 people. The-- we remain on the ground about 500, 600 people. The rest they marched them back to Treblinka to the [? ovens. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: The people who opened the cars, the railroad cars.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were SS.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And the people who were on the platform, where there any prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Only SS?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only-- that was not-- This was not at Treblinka. It was the Treblinka, but if they would have to go over there where the guards, there is a different--

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Different-- you know, different direction completely. So we were-- because they didn't go in straight to the ovens. They go in-- first of all, they went to the other Treblinka, the [? arbeitz ?] Treblinka, so they have to stop in a different area and then shuttle them over to there. And then they-- then they-- after the selection, they took the older people which they selected to be gassed in the other Treblinka. And the rest, we remained in the-- sitting on the-- on the ground. So they picked up people. They came over.

speaker: interviewee
text: We stood up. What are you doing? I accidentally said I'm a carpenter. A carpenter. All right. I was young, you know. And this side, the-- the other more older people with women, they put on a different side. And they selected this-- the group which they have to go back to the-- to the Treblinka for the gassing. They were-- put them together and they marched-- marched them away. They march them over to Treblinka.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we found out this group, you see, in the morning, they had to take him a group to Malchin. That's a station, Malchin, to Malchin to work there. Also the coal, [INAUDIBLE], doing all kind of work.

speaker: interviewee
text: Instead, to bring him back, these people which they took out from the Treblinka where I was there. Instead to bring them back there, they took them straight down to the ovens, and they liquidate them. You see? They didn't see-- so there, they prepared some food for them, the cook, the soup, whatever there us. But this soup and this everything, that this group what remains there sitting on the-- on the-- on the ground, when they left for the other Treblinka for the liquidation, so they took us into a barrack. [? Already. ?] This barrack was known as the death barrack. What was it called in German?

speaker: interviewee
text: The [GERMAN]. And so this-- the-- the people, which they are supposed to come back and the stay there, they didn't came back. They took them right there. You know, it just that moment when-- when we divided-- they divided ourselves. They had their friend, and they had their brother and their sister. And they had their father. And they had their mother. This-- the cry which was-- went down there. There was-- not even to-- to-- to tell you how-- how tragic it was that moment. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
